input.content:
---
name: project_pr1227_sync_state_design
description: Design direction agreed during PR #1227 review — sync "in sync / out of sync" is per-slot across name AND every managed credential type, with smart granular writes. Slot = user_id invariant preserved.
metadata:
  type: project
---

Settled during PR #1227 discussion (June 2026). When the LCM sync layer expands beyond PIN-only:

**In-sync predicate per slot** is the conjunction of:
- `lock.user.name == truncated(config.name, max_user_name_length)` — trivially equal on User Code CC locks because both sides are None
- For each managed credential type T: `lock.user.credential_of_type(T) == config.credential_for(T)` (today only PIN; future PASSWORD etc.)

Slot is "out of sync" until every field matches. Sync writes only the deltas — name-only via `async_set_user`, credential-only via `async_set_credential` per mismatching type. `_put_credential` is the "both/create" combinator and stays the rollback-bearing path.

**Slot = user_id invariant kept.** LCM enforces the convention "slot N = user_id N" — adopt-by-existing-owner (the Matter pattern) is NOT brought to Z-Wave even though the User Credential CC would allow it. Reasons:
- Matches the existing 1:1:1 mental model.
- An orphan user with no credentials (left over when LCM normalizes out-of-band state) is harmless and visible in HA's Z-Wave UI.
- Simpler debugging — slot number always equals user_id.

**Multi-credential user adoption.** When LCM manages slot 1 and the lock has user 1 with PIN + RFID (set out-of-band), LCM:
- Adopts user 1 (cache hit on `get_user_cached(user_id=1)`)
- Updates only the PIN, leaves the RFID untouched
- On clear: removes only the PIN; `_drop_credential` keeps the user because RFID remains
- Eventually deletes the user when its last credential is removed (existing seam logic)

LCM manages credentials it knows about; everything else is the user's business. NO sweep of non-managed credential types.

**Config schema direction** (when password support lands):
```yaml
1:
  name: Alice
  pin: "1234"
  password: "hunter2"  # capability-gated, only shown if max_user_name_length>0 and PASSWORD in credential_types
  enabled_condition: ...
```
Slot per user; each slot can carry one credential per supported type. Polymorphic-slot vs type-scoped-slot debate resolved by user as "slot per user, keep it simple". See [[project_password_credential_expansion]].

**Why:** the alternative — clear non-PIN credentials on takeover — was rejected as overreach. The right principle: LCM is the source of truth for the credential types it manages, not for the user record as a whole.

**How to apply:** when expanding the sync layer beyond PIN-only, replace `dict[int, SlotCredential]` "desired vs actual" comparisons with `dict[int, User]` (or equivalent) so the comparison sees name + every credential type. Granular write primitives already exist (`async_set_user`, `async_set_credential`); the work is in the sync manager, not the providers. See [[project_password_credential_expansion]] for the broader roadmap and [[project_zwave_credential_cc_abstraction]] for the driver-level CC dispatch that makes this work transparently across 500/700/800-series locks.

---
name: project_pr1227_sync_state_design
description: Design direction agreed during PR
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: f007f8e0-8e15-49f8-9b78-ca4730e9a56f
---

Settled during PR #1227 discussion (June 2026). When the LCM sync layer expands beyond PIN-only:

**In-sync predicate per slot** is the conjunction of:
- `lock.user.name == truncated(config.name, max_user_name_length)` — trivially equal on User Code CC locks because both sides are None
- For each managed credential type T: `lock.user.credential_of_type(T) == config.credential_for(T)` (today only PIN; future PASSWORD etc.)

Slot is "out of sync" until every field matches. Sync writes only the deltas — name-only via `async_set_user`, credential-only via `async_set_credential` per mismatching type. `_put_credential` is the "both/create" combinator and stays the rollback-bearing path.

**Slot = user_id invariant kept.** LCM enforces the convention "slot N = user_id N" — adopt-by-existing-owner (the Matter pattern) is NOT brought to Z-Wave even though the User Credential CC would allow it. Reasons:
- Matches the existing 1:1:1 mental model.
- An orphan user with no credentials (left over when LCM normalizes out-of-band state) is harmless and visible in HA's Z-Wave UI.
- Simpler debugging — slot number always equals user_id.

**Multi-credential user adoption.** When LCM manages slot 1 and the lock has user 1 with PIN + RFID (set out-of-band), LCM:
- Adopts user 1 (cache hit on `get_user_cached(user_id=1)`)
- Updates only the PIN, leaves the RFID untouched
- On clear: removes only the PIN; `_drop_credential` keeps the user because RFID remains
- Eventually deletes the user when its last credential is removed (existing seam logic)

LCM manages credentials it knows about; everything else is the user's business. NO sweep of non-managed credential types.

**Config schema direction** (when password support lands):
```yaml
1:
  name: Alice
  pin: "1234"
  password: "hunter2"  # capability-gated, only shown if max_user_name_length>0 and PASSWORD in credential_types
  enabled_condition: ...
```
Slot per user; each slot can carry one credential per supported type. Polymorphic-slot vs type-scoped-slot debate resolved by user as "slot per user, keep it simple". See [[project_password_credential_expansion]].

**Why:** the alternative — clear non-PIN credentials on takeover — was rejected as overreach. The right principle: LCM is the source of truth for the credential types it manages, not for the user record as a whole.

**How to apply:** when expanding the sync layer beyond PIN-only, replace `dict[int, SlotCredential]` "desired vs actual" comparisons with `dict[int, User]` (or equivalent) so the comparison sees name + every credential type. Granular write primitives already exist (`async_set_user`, `async_set_credential`); the work is in the sync manager, not the providers. See [[project_password_credential_expansion]] for the broader roadmap and [[project_zwave_credential_cc_abstraction]] for the driver-level CC dispatch that makes this work transparently across 500/700/800-series locks.

---
name: feedback_trace_through_layers
description: When reasoning about Z-Wave (or any layered system) behavior, trace through every layer of the stack — driver, server, client library, integration helper — before drawing architectural conclusions. Assumptions about what an abstract API "must mean" routinely go wrong without the trace.
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

When working on Z-Wave-related questions, trace through ALL of: the Python client (`zwave_js_server`), the Node.js server (`zwave-js-server`), and the driver itself (`node-zwave-js`). All three are checked out at `~/projects/`. **Pull latest in each repo before searching** — local checkouts can be on feature branches or behind the released version that's installed in the venv.

**Why:** Lost real time during PR #1227 review by reasoning from the surface API alone. I assumed `node.access_control.is_supported()` meant "User Credential CC supported" because the namespace name (`access_control`) sounded like it. Wrong — tracing to the driver showed the API is exposed for either User Credential CC OR User Code CC, and dispatch happens INSIDE the API. I was about to propose a big backend-split refactor for a problem that didn't exist. The user caught it with a single pointed question.

**How to apply:**
- Before drawing an architectural conclusion from an abstract API ("we need to do X because the API requires Y"), trace through every layer. The thing you assume the API requires may be handled one layer down.
- For Z-Wave specifically:
  - HA integration: `~/projects/lock_code_manager/.venv/lib/python3.14/site-packages/homeassistant/components/zwave_js/`
  - Python client: `~/projects/zwave-js-server-python/`
  - Node.js server: `~/projects/zwave-js-server/`
  - Driver: `~/projects/node-zwave-js/`
- The venv version often LEADS the local main checkout — installed packages may have features not yet in your local main, or behind tags you need to check out explicitly (see the v15.23.4 dance for `accessControl`).
- The user's instinct "the whole point of the abstract API is to not have to know this" is correct — if the abstraction looks leaky, the leak is probably in your reading, not the API.

content.text:
Traced through. **Events are identical across CCs — but the write ordering isn't.** Push subscriptions are safe; one provider-level guard is still needed.

## Events: same for both CCs

`AccessControlAPI` emits the same event names regardless of which CC backs the operation:
- `"user added"` / `"user modified"` / `"user deleted"`
- `"credential added"` / `"credential modified"` / `"credential deleted"`

For UC the events are synthesized inside the API methods themselves; for U3C they're driven by report handling. Same payload shape: `{userId, credentialType, credentialSlot, data?}`. PR 1227's push subscription wires those three credential events and `_pin_state(args.data)` handles the present/absent value uniformly. **No push subscription split needed.**

One nuance worth knowing — UC's `deleteCredential` fires BOTH `"credential deleted"` AND `"user deleted"` (because UC ties them together), so our handler will see two events for a single clear. Currently harmless because our handlers are idempotent per slot.

## Reads / capabilities: same shape

Everything from `getUsersCached`, `getAllCredentialsCached`, `getCredentialCapabilitiesCached`, `getUserCapabilitiesCached` returns the same Python types regardless of CC. UC just populates them with `max_user_name_length=0`, names=None, `credential_types={PIN: {...}}` (PIN-only). **No read-path split needed.**

## Writes: the one real asymmetry

`AccessControlAPI.setUser()` on a UC lock (`feature-apis/AccessControl.ts:540` area):

```typescript
const existingCode = this.getValue<string>(UserCodeCCValues.userCode(userId)...);
if (!existingCode) {
    throw new ZWaveError(
        "User Code CC requires a credential to be set before modifying the user. Use setCredential() first.",
        ZWaveErrorCodes.Argument_Invalid,
    );
}
```

UC has no concept of a userless user — the user IS the code. So:
- **Empty slot, U3C lock**: `_put_credential` → `async_set_user` (creates user) → `async_set_credential` (attaches PIN). Works.
- **Empty slot, UC lock**: `_put_credential` → `async_set_user` **throws** → no PIN written. **Regression.**
- **Occupied slot, UC lock**: `async_set_user` works (code already exists, can re-send to update active/status). `async_set_credential` overwrites the value. Works.

So the seam's "set user first, then credential" ordering breaks on UC for the create case specifically. The driver doesn't paper this over because it's a real semantic difference, not a CC-API difference.

## Fix

Smallest change: have `async_set_user` skip the driver call on UC locks. Detect UC structurally — UC's capabilities are `max_user_name_length == 0` AND the credential-types map has PIN only with no other entries. (We already cache `max_user_name_length`; the PIN-only check is one capabilities-read away.)

Sketch:

```python
@property
def _is_legacy_user_code_cc(self) -> bool:
    """
    UC vs U3C heuristic from capabilities.

    User Code CC locks always report max_user_name_length=0 and expose
    only the PIN credential type. A U3C lock could theoretically also
    report 0-length names, but it would still advertise the full
    credential vocabulary — the conjunction is the practical signal.
    """
    return (
        self._max_user_name_length == 0
        and self._supported_credential_types == {CredentialType.PIN}
    )

async def async_set_user(self, user: User) -> SetUserResult:
    ...resolve caps as today...
    if self._is_legacy_user_code_cc:
        # UC: the user record doesn't exist independently of the credential.
        # Skip the driver call; async_set_credential will create the user
        # implicitly. Report created=False so _put_credential doesn't try
        # to roll back a user it didn't actually write.
        return SetUserResult(user_id=user.user_id, created=False)
    # ...existing U3C path with name truncation + lock_helpers.async_set_user...
```

Trade-off: we lose the ability to update `active`/`userType` on UC locks. LCM doesn't model `userType` today and treats slots as always-active, so the practical impact is zero. If we ever wanted active-toggling on UC, we'd add a "credential exists?" pre-check and only call setUser when it does (matter.py-style).

Tests:
- Add a UC-fixture test that walks through `_put_credential` on an empty slot — should succeed with one `set_credential` call and zero `set_user` calls.
- Existing U3C tests stay green because the guard only fires when `max_user_name_length == 0`.

## Summary

| Surface | UC vs U3C | LCM split needed? |
|---|---|---|
| Push events | Identical names + shape | No |
| Capabilities | Same fields, UC has 0/PIN-only | No |
| Read paths (`getUsers*`, `getAllCredentials*`) | Same shape | No |
| `setCredential` | Same call site, works without prior `setUser` | No |
| `setUser` empty-slot | UC throws | **Yes — provider-level guard, ~10 lines** |
| `deleteCredential` | UC fires extra `"user deleted"` event | No (idempotent) |
| `deleteUser` | Both work; UC clears the underlying code | No |

So: one guarded branch in `async_set_user`, plus the structural UC-detection helper. Belongs in PR 1227 with the setup probe. Want me to wire it in?

We skip the UC one since the user doesn't matter, but we enforce a min of 6 (greater of the two) and a max of 10 (least of the two)

input.content:
---
name: project_lcd_capability_aggregation
description: Future PR — least-common-denominator capability aggregation across multiple locks in a config entry. Enforce limits at the text entity level for both PIN length and user name length.
metadata:
  type: project
---

When a Lock Code Manager config entry covers multiple locks of different capabilities, the effective per-slot constraints should be aggregated as the **least common denominator** so a single PIN/name works across all of them.

**Aggregation rules** (per credential type):

- **min_length** = `max(lock.min_length for lock in non_skipped_locks)` — every lock must accept it, so take the highest floor.
- **max_length** = `min(lock.max_length for lock in non_skipped_locks)` — every lock must accept it, so take the lowest ceiling.
- **User name max length** = same rule (min of all locks' max_user_name_length, ignoring locks with 0).
- **Skip locks where the capability doesn't apply** before aggregating. Example: User Code CC locks (max_user_name_length=0) are skipped when computing the name-length aggregate — they don't have names, so they don't constrain the floor/ceiling. Equivalent skip-rule for credential types: a lock that doesn't support PASSWORD doesn't contribute to PASSWORD length aggregation; it's just not part of the PASSWORD group.
- **Empty aggregate is a meaningful state** — "no lock in this config entry can hold a PASSWORD" means the password field shouldn't appear on slots. Treat absent capability as "type not available", not "type unconstrained".

**Worked example** (user-provided): three Z-Wave locks in one config entry.
- Lock A: User Code CC (skip for names; for PIN min/max=4/10).
- Lock B: User Credential CC, PIN min/max = 4/10, name max = 8.
- Lock C: User Credential CC, PIN min/max = 6/12, name max = 12.

Effective per-slot:
- PIN: min = max(4, 4, 6) = 6, max = min(10, 10, 12) = 10.
- Name: max = min(8, 12) = 8 (Lock A skipped).

**Enforcement point**: the text entity itself, on both the PIN entity and the name entity. Use HA `text` platform's `native_min`/`native_max` to surface limits at the UI layer so the user gets immediate feedback rather than a write-time failure. The base orchestration also validates server-side as a defense in depth, but the entity attributes are the primary user signal.

**Why:** before this aggregation, the user could legally configure a PIN that one lock accepts and another rejects — the sync layer would then loop on the rejecting lock with `CodeRejectedError`. Pushing the constraint into the entity attribute makes the conflict impossible to express in the first place.

**How to apply:** ships AFTER the password expansion (see [[project_password_credential_expansion]]) and the sync-state name reconciliation (see [[project_pr1227_sync_state_design]]). Belongs in a future PR alongside those. Sequence: (1) UC guard + setup probe land in PR #1227. (2) Backend wiring for multiple credential types lands when password support is added. (3) THIS aggregation layer lands when entities are wired against the multi-lock capability surface, gating per-credential-type fields by whether any non-skipped lock supports that type.

file.content:
# TODO

## User-Facing Features

- **Config flow: conflicting integration detection** — Warn if Keymaster or other
  code management integrations are detected at setup.
- **Clear all unmanaged codes** — Button or service to clear unmanaged code slots
  on a lock.
- **Bulk operations** — Enable/disable multiple slots at once.
- **Import/export slot configuration**
- **PIN validation** — Warn on simple PINs ("1234", "0000"), duplicate PINs
  across slots, format violations against lock requirements, and slot conflicts
  across config entries.
- **Service actions:**
  - `generate_pin` — auto-generate secure PIN
- **Custom Jinja templates** — Ship `.jinja` macros for LCM entity resolution
  (e.g. `lcm_slot_entities(config_entry_id, slot_num)`). Auto-install to
  `custom_templates/` during setup.

## Providers

**Future:** Nuki, SimpliSafe (has `async_get_pins()` in simplipy — full
read/write, name-based, max 4 user PINs), SwitchBot, SmartThings

**Cannot support:** esphome (no API), august/yale/yalexs_ble/yale_smart_alarm
(library limitations), ISY994 (pyisy has no code read support)

### Matter provider

- **Known Aqara U300 limitations** (discovered during live testing 2026-04-18):
  - User names with spaces rejected (500 error from `set_lock_user`)
  - Status `unknown(133)` when setting credential on occupied slot without clearing
  - Lock disconnects from Thread network after repeated HA restarts (needs battery
    pull + Matter server restart to recover)

## Architecture Considerations

- **Event-driven vs optimistic push updates** — Matter, Z-Wave JS, and
  Zigbee2MQTT all need optimistic pushes from set/clear methods. Z-Wave JS
  needs them to avoid sync loops with stale cache reads. Matter needs them
  because PINs are write-only. Zigbee2MQTT uses MQTT QoS 0 which has no
  delivery confirmation. Removing optimistic pushes is not viable for any
  push-based provider. Event-only updates would leave a latency window where
  the coordinator has stale data, triggering unnecessary re-sync attempts.

- **Coordinator-owned sync managers** — Move sync manager lifecycle from binary
  sensor entities to coordinator (survives entity recreation during config
  updates).

## Code Quality

- **Dual storage pattern** — Simplify `data` + `options` config entry pattern.
  Document when to use each.
- **TypedDict for slot config** — Define `class SlotConfig(TypedDict)` with
  `pin: NotRequired[str]`, `enabled: bool`, `name: NotRequired[str]`,
  `entity_id: NotRequired[str]` to replace `dict[str, Any]` for slot inner
  dicts. Gives pyright real signal on slot reads/writes. Would let
  `EntryConfig.slots` be typed `Mapping[int, SlotConfig]`.
- **`coordinator.data` typing** — Currently `dict[int, str | SlotCode]`,
  already int-keyed. `binary_sensor.py:281` and `entity.py:193` cast
  defensively against `self.slot_num`'s type variance — could be cleaned
  up once entities consistently carry int slot_num end-to-end.
- **Other internal dict boundaries** — `websocket.py:401/442/524/529`
  cast into websocket-internal lookup dicts; `__init__.py:561` casts at
  a callback boundary. Same broader "typed slot_num everywhere" theme as
  the EntryConfig migration; resolved by the SlotConfig TypedDict work
  above plus typed callback signatures.
- **Entity registry change detection** — Warn if LCM entity IDs change (reload
  required).

## Testing

- **Live testing** — Remaining scenarios not yet validated on any lock:
  - Multiple config entries sharing the same lock (conflict detection)
  - Hard refresh drift detection (verify poll catches out-of-band changes)
  - Config flow re-add (picks up existing codes on lock)

## Frontend

- Test visual editor for both cards (post-redesign).

## Process

- `CLAUDE.md` points to `AGENTS.md`; update `AGENTS.md` after architecture
  changes.
- Review TODOs after completing current work, when starting new features, during
  refactoring sessions, or on `/todos`.

content.text:
Yes, I think it's a good idea — with a couple of edges to think about up front.

## My take

**Strongest part**: the "skip locks where the capability doesn't apply" rule. Without it you'd be stuck choosing between constraining everything to the UC-level (no names ever) or pretending UC locks have names (they don't). The skip rule lets each capability aggregate over only the locks that actually have it, which is the right semantic.

**The aggregation rule is correct for numeric bounds** (min via max, max via min, both monotone). It generalizes cleanly to PIN length, name length, slot counts, and any future numeric capability.

**Where I'd want explicit design choices before shipping**:

1. **Impossible aggregates**. If lock A says 4–6 and lock B says 8–12, the LCD is min=8, max=6 — empty. Fail-loud at setup with a config error ("these locks have incompatible PIN length requirements; split them into separate config entries") is better than silently clamping to one or the other. Worth deciding the failure shape now.

2. **Different shapes per capability kind**. Numeric bounds aggregate as min/max. Boolean flags (e.g. `supports_learn` for RFID — if relevant later) aggregate as AND across locks that support the type. Type availability (does any lock support PASSWORD?) is a disjunction over the contributing-locks set. So "LCD" is really three rules dressed up as one — easy enough but worth documenting per type.

3. **Per-type capability gating is separate from length aggregation**. If two locks support PASSWORD and one doesn't, you've got a choice: hide the password field entirely (safe, restrictive), or show it and have sync only write to supporting locks (flexible, possibly confusing). My preference: show it with an attribute or footnote indicating which locks will receive the value — but this is a UX call, not an engineering one.

4. **Reactivity on lock add/remove**. Adding a tighter-bound lock to an existing config entry can invalidate previously-valid configured PINs/names. Plan: re-validate slot configs at setup against the new aggregate, mark invalid slots disabled with a clear warning, surface in the UI.

Bottom line: the principle is right and the design generalizes well; the failure modes (empty aggregate, mixed type support) are where it earns or loses its UX.

## Proposed TODO.md entries

input.new_string:
## Architecture Considerations

- **Event-driven vs optimistic push updates** — Matter, Z-Wave JS, and
  Zigbee2MQTT all need optimistic pushes from set/clear methods. Z-Wave JS
  needs them to avoid sync loops with stale cache reads. Matter needs them
  because PINs are write-only. Zigbee2MQTT uses MQTT QoS 0 which has no
  delivery confirmation. Removing optimistic pushes is not viable for any
  push-based provider. Event-only updates would leave a latency window where
  the coordinator has stale data, triggering unnecessary re-sync attempts.

- **Coordinator-owned sync managers** — Move sync manager lifecycle from binary
  sensor entities to coordinator (survives entity recreation during config
  updates).

- **Expand sync "in sync" predicate to include user name + every managed
  credential type** — Today sync compares PIN only. With names (User
  Credential CC) and future credential types (PASSWORD, etc.), a slot
  should be "out of sync" if any field — name, PIN, or any managed
  non-PIN credential — drifts from config. Smart writes only the deltas:
  name-only via `async_set_user`, credential-only via `async_set_credential`
  per mismatching type, both via `_put_credential` for the create path.
  Trivially-equal name comparison on User Code CC locks (both sides
  None) keeps the same code path safe for legacy locks. Requires the
  coordinator/sync state to carry `User` rather than `dict[int,
  SlotCredential]` so name is visible to the sync predicate.

- **Least-common-denominator capability aggregation across locks in a
  config entry** — When a config entry covers multiple locks, aggregate
  per-credential-type limits as the LCD so a single configured value
  works on all of them. Numeric bounds: `min_length = max(...)`,
  `max_length = min(...)` over locks that support the type. Skip locks
  where the capability doesn't apply (e.g. User Code CC locks contribute
  nothing to name-length aggregation because UC has no names). Type
  availability is disjunctive (any lock supports PASSWORD → field
  appears; the sync layer per-lock decides whether to write).
  Failure modes to design: empty aggregate (min > max — fail-loud at
  setup with a config error, suggest splitting locks into separate
  entries); recompute on lock add/remove with re-validation of existing
  slot configs. Enforce at the `text` entity level via `native_min` /
  `native_max` for both PIN and name. Lands after the password
  expansion + name-reconciliation sync work above.

- **Expand beyond PIN: add password credential type** — Z-Wave User
  Credential CC and Matter DoorLock both expose `PASSWORD` natively.
  Slot config schema becomes `{name, pin, password, ...}` — slot per
  user, multiple credential types per slot. Capability-gated per slot
  by `max_user_name_length > 0` (real users) and
  `credential_types[PASSWORD]` advertised by at least one lock in the
  entry. Base orchestration is already type-parametric
  (`_project_users_to_slots(credential_type)`); the work is config
  schema, frontend (separate text entity per type per slot or
  capability-driven dropdown), and sync layer multiplexing per type.
  Sequence: lands after the sync-state expansion above and the LCD
  aggregation, since both are prerequisites for a coherent multi-type
  UX.

## Code Quality

- **Dual storage pattern** — Simplify `data` + `options` config entry pattern.

- **TypedDict for slot config** — Define `class SlotConfig(TypedDict)` with

  `entity_id: NotRequired[str]` to replace `dict[str, Any]` for slot inner
  dicts. Gives pyright real signal on slot reads/writes. Would let
  `EntryConfig.slots` be typed `Mapping[int, SlotConfig]`.
- **`coordinator.data` typing** — Currently `dict[int, str | SlotCode]`,
  already int-keyed. `binary_sensor.py:281` and `entity.py:193` cast
  defensively against `self.slot_num`'s type variance — could be cleaned
  up once entities consistently carry int slot_num end-to-end.
- **Other internal dict boundaries** — `websocket.py:401/442/524/529`
  cast into websocket-internal lookup dicts; `__init__.py:561` casts at
  a callback boundary. Same broader "typed slot_num everywhere" theme as
  the EntryConfig migration; resolved by the SlotConfig TypedDict work
  above plus typed callback signatures.
- **Entity registry change detection** — Warn if LCM entity IDs change (reload
  required).

## Testing

- **Live testing** — Remaining scenarios not yet validated on any lock:
  - Multiple config entries sharing the same lock (conflict detection)
  - Hard refresh drift detection (verify poll catches out-of-band changes)
  - Config flow re-add (picks up existing codes on lock)

## Frontend

- Test visual editor for both cards (post-redesign).

## Process

- `CLAUDE.md` points to `AGENTS.md`; update `AGENTS.md` after architecture
  changes.
- Review TODOs after completing current work, when starting new features, during
  refactoring sessions, or on `/todos`.

lines: sensor entities to coordinator (survives entity recreation during config
+- **Expand sync "in sync" predicate to include user name + every managed
+  credential type** — Today sync compares PIN only. With names (User
+  Credential CC) and future credential types (PASSWORD, etc.), a slot
+  should be "out of sync" if any field — name, PIN, or any managed
+  non-PIN credential — drifts from config. Smart writes only the deltas:
+  per mismatching type, both via `_put_credential` for the create path.
+  Trivially-equal name comparison on User Code CC locks (both sides
+  None) keeps the same code path safe for legacy locks. Requires the
+  coordinator/sync state to carry `User` rather than `dict[int,
+  SlotCredential]` so name is visible to the sync predicate.
+- **Least-common-denominator capability aggregation across locks in a
+  config entry** — When a config entry covers multiple locks, aggregate
+  per-credential-type limits as the LCD so a single configured value
+  works on all of them. Numeric bounds: `min_length = max(...)`,
+  `max_length = min(...)` over locks that support the type. Skip locks
+  where the capability doesn't apply (e.g. User Code CC locks contribute
+  nothing to name-length aggregation because UC has no names). Type
+  availability is disjunctive (any lock supports PASSWORD → field
+  appears; the sync layer per-lock decides whether to write).
+  Failure modes to design: empty aggregate (min > max — fail-loud at
+  setup with a config error, suggest splitting locks into separate
+  entries); recompute on lock add/remove with re-validation of existing
+  slot configs. Enforce at the `text` entity level via `native_min` /
+  `native_max` for both PIN and name. Lands after the password
+- **Expand beyond PIN: add password credential type** — Z-Wave User
+  Credential CC and Matter DoorLock both expose `PASSWORD` natively.
+  Slot config schema becomes `{name, pin, password, ...}` — slot per
+  user, multiple credential types per slot. Capability-gated per slot
+  `credential_types[PASSWORD]` advertised by at least one lock in the
+  entry. Base orchestration is already type-parametric
+  schema, frontend (separate text entity per type per slot or
+  capability-driven dropdown), and sync layer multiplexing per type.
+  Sequence: lands after the sync-state expansion above and the LCD
+  aggregation, since both are prerequisites for a coherent multi-type
- **Dual storage pattern** — Simplify `data` + `options` config entry pattern.

    @property
    def supports_native_users(self) -> bool:
        """Return True: this provider implements the credential primitives."""
        return True

    def _pin_state(self, data: str | bytes | None) -> SlotCredential:
        """Project Z-Wave credential data to a SlotCredential (readable when present)."""
        if not data:
            return SlotCredential.unreadable()
        # CredentialData.data is str | bytes; decode bytes so a Personal
        # Identification Number is the digit string, not "b'1234'".
        return SlotCredential.known(data if isinstance(data, str) else data.decode())

    async def async_get_users(self) -> list[User]:
        """
        Read every user and all of their credentials from the lock.

        Returns users carrying every credential type the domain model can
        represent (Personal Identification Number, Radio Frequency
        Identification, Near Field Communication, password, face,
        fingerprint). The base orchestration filters to Personal
        Identification Number at the slot-projection layer via
        ``user.pin_credentials``, so this method does no type-specific
        filtering -- direct callers see the full picture without an
        extra read. Z-Wave credential types with no domain equivalent
        (BLE, UWB, DESFIRE, unspecified/eye/hand biometrics) are
        dropped.
        """
        try:
            users = await self.node.access_control.get_users_cached()
            credentials = await self.node.access_control.get_all_credentials_cached()

255

257	        ``user.user_id`` (which the helper always honors, never auto-allocating
258	        here), not from the helper's return value, so the base orchestration
259	        can roll back a user this call newly created.

262	        # not yet been called (or the cache is stale), fetch capabilities

267	                    self.node

271	                # a name guard, but leave the cache unset so the next call
272	                # retries. Caching a 0 sentinel here would silently strip
273	                # all user names for the lifetime of the provider instance
274	                # after a single transient disconnect.

276	            else:

278	        max_name_len: int = self._max_user_name_length or 0

async def async_get_capabilities(self) -> LockCapabilities:
        """Report the lock's user/credential capabilities."""
        try:
            caps = await lock_helpers.async_get_credential_capabilities(self.node)
        except BaseZwaveJSServerError as err:
            raise LockDisconnected(f"get capabilities failed: {err}") from err
        except HomeAssistantError as err:
            raise LockOperationFailed(f"get capabilities failed: {err}") from err
        # Cache max name length and supported types for async_set_user; the
        # conjunction is what ``_uses_user_code_cc`` keys on.
        self._max_user_name_length = caps.get("max_user_name_length", 0)
        pin = caps["supported_credential_types"].get(_PIN_TYPE_STR)
        credential_types = (
            {
                CredentialType.PIN: CredentialTypeCapability(
                    num_slots=pin["num_slots"],
                    min_length=pin["min_length"],
                    max_length=pin["max_length"],
                    supports_learn=pin["supports_learn"],
                )
            }
            if pin
            else {}
        )
        self._supported_credential_types = frozenset(credential_types)
        return LockCapabilities(
            supports_user_management=caps["supports_user_management"],
            max_users=caps["max_users"],
            credential_types=credential_types,
        )

    @property
    def _uses_user_code_cc(self) -> bool:
        """
        Return whether the lock is being served via the legacy User Code CC.

        node-zwave-js v15.23.4+ exposes a unified ``accessControl`` API
        backed by either User Credential CC (newer 700/800-series locks)
        or the legacy User Code CC (older 500-series locks); read paths
        are transparent across both, but the write semantics differ. In
        particular, User Code CC's ``setUser`` requires an existing
        credential because the user IS the code, so the base
        orchestration's "set user first, then credential" sequence has
        to be adapted for that backend.

        The unified API does not expose the underlying CC directly. The
        practical signal is the capability conjunction: User Code CC
        always reports ``max_user_name_length=0`` (no names) and only
        the Personal Identification Number credential type. A User
        Credential CC lock that happened to report 0-length names would
        still advertise the broader credential-type vocabulary, so the
        conjunction is what disambiguates.

        Returns ``False`` when capabilities have not yet been resolved
        -- caller defaults to the User Credential CC path, which is the
        more common case today and degrades gracefully if the heuristic
        is wrong (the driver surfaces a clear ``ZWaveError`` we map to
        ``LockOperationFailed``).
        """
        if (
            self._max_user_name_length is None
            or self._supported_credential_types is None
        ):
            return False
        return self._max_user_name_length == 0 and self._supported_credential_types == {
            CredentialType.PIN
        }

+        is wrong (the driver surfaces a clear ``ZWaveError`` we map to
async def async_set_user(self, user: User) -> SetUserResult:
Create or update the lock user; report whether it was created.

# Resolve the max user name length and supported credential types.
        # The cache is populated here on first call (or by
        # ``async_get_capabilities``); on capabilities-fetch failure the
        # cache stays unset so the next call retries instead of getting
        # stuck at a sentinel that would silently drop all user names.
        if self._max_user_name_length is None:
            # Best-effort prime; defer to async_get_capabilities so the
            # supported-types cache is populated in lockstep with
            # max_user_name_length. Failures are non-fatal -- proceed for
            # this call without a name guard.
            with contextlib.suppress(LockDisconnected, LockOperationFailed):
                await self.async_get_capabilities()
        # User Code CC's ``setUser`` cannot run before a credential
        # exists (the user IS the code), so on a UC lock the seam's
        # "set user first, then credential" sequence has to skip the
        # set-user write entirely. ``async_set_credential`` creates the
        # user implicitly when it writes the code. ``created=False``
        # because there is no separate user record to roll back if the
        # credential write fails.
        if self._uses_user_code_cc:
            return SetUserResult(user_id=user.user_id, created=False)

file.content:
return ZWAVE_JS_DOMAIN

    def _clear_listeners(self) -> None:
        """Unsubscribe and clear all HA event bus listeners."""
        for listener in self._listeners:
            listener()
        self._listeners.clear()

    async def async_setup(self, config_entry: ConfigEntry) -> None:
        """
        Set up lock by provider.

        Idempotent: clears existing listeners before re-registering.
        """
        self._clear_listeners()
        self._listeners.append(
            self.hass.bus.async_listen(
                ZWAVE_JS_NOTIFICATION_EVENT,
                self._handle_zwave_js_event,
                self._zwave_js_event_filter,
            )
        )

    async def async_unload(self, remove_permanently: bool) -> None:
        """Unload lock."""
        self._clear_listeners()
        await super().async_unload(remove_permanently)

    async def async_is_integration_connected(self) -> bool:
        """Return whether the Z-Wave JS client is connected."""

input.new_string:
async def async_setup(self, config_entry: ConfigEntry) -> None:
        """
        Set up lock by provider.

        Prime the capability cache so ``_uses_user_code_cc`` is resolved
        before the first write, and surface a clear typed error if the
        lock exposes neither User Credential CC nor User Code CC (i.e.
        the unified ``accessControl`` API is not available at all --
        effectively never for a real Z-Wave lock, but worth catching
        cleanly rather than as an opaque attribute error downstream).

        Idempotent: clears existing listeners before re-registering.
        """
        self._clear_listeners()
        # Prime the capability cache. This is the same call ``async_set_user``
        # would make lazily; doing it at setup means the User Code CC
        # heuristic is ready before the first write and lets us translate
        # the "neither CC supported" failure into a typed LCM error rather
        # than letting a downstream ``node.access_control`` access raise.
        try:
            await self.async_get_capabilities()
        except LockOperationFailed as err:
            # ``lock_helpers`` surfaces a structured error when the
            # endpoint exposes no access-control API at all. Translate to
            # an LCM provider error so the integration fails setup cleanly
            # with a message a user can act on.
            raise LockOperationFailed(
                f"{self.lock.entity_id}: capabilities probe failed -- this "
                "Z-Wave lock does not expose User Credential CC or User Code "
                f"CC via the unified access-control API. ({err})"
            ) from err
        self._listeners.append(
            self.hass.bus.async_listen(
                ZWAVE_JS_NOTIFICATION_EVENT,
                self._handle_zwave_js_event,
                self._zwave_js_event_filter,
            )
        )

content.text: Let me tighten the LCD entry per your feedback and add Option B.

input.old_string:
- **Least-common-denominator capability aggregation across locks in a
  config entry** — When a config entry covers multiple locks, aggregate
  per-credential-type limits as the LCD so a single configured value
  works on all of them. Numeric bounds: `min_length = max(...)`,
  `max_length = min(...)` over locks that support the type. Skip locks
  where the capability doesn't apply (e.g. User Code CC locks contribute
  nothing to name-length aggregation because UC has no names). Type
  availability is disjunctive (any lock supports PASSWORD → field
  appears; the sync layer per-lock decides whether to write).
  Failure modes to design: empty aggregate (min > max — fail-loud at
  setup with a config error, suggest splitting locks into separate
  entries); recompute on lock add/remove with re-validation of existing
  slot configs. Enforce at the `text` entity level via `native_min` /
  `native_max` for both PIN and name. Lands after the password
  expansion + name-reconciliation sync work above.
input.new_string:
- **Least-common-denominator capability aggregation across locks in a
  config entry** — When a config entry covers multiple locks, aggregate
  per-credential-type limits as the LCD so a single configured value
  works on all of them. Rules per capability kind:
  - Numeric bounds: `min_length = max(...)`, `max_length = min(...)`
    across the locks that support the type. Skip locks where the
    capability doesn't apply (e.g. User Code CC locks contribute
    nothing to name-length aggregation because UC has no names).
  - Type availability: any lock supports the type → the field appears
    on the slot config; the sync layer per-lock decides whether to
    write. When no lock supports the type, hide the field entirely.
  - Boolean feature flags (e.g. `supports_learn` for RFID): AND across
    the supporting locks.
  Empty aggregate (e.g. PIN min > max because one lock requires <=6 and
  another requires >=8) must be a hard config flow failure surfaced in
  both the initial config flow and the option flow ("these locks have
  incompatible PIN length requirements; split them into separate config
  entries"). On lock add/remove, re-validate existing slot configs
  against the recomputed aggregate and disable any slots whose stored
  values fall outside the new bounds, with a clear repair-suggestion
  notification. Enforce at the `text` entity level via `native_min` /
  `native_max` for both PIN and name. Lands after the password
  expansion + name-reconciliation sync work above.

- **Option B: entity-type scoping per credential type ("slot mappings")**
  — Today the base orchestration carries Option A (parametric per-type
  projection via `_project_users_to_slots(credential_type)`). The
  open question is whether the coordinator/entities themselves should
  become type-scoped from the top — separate slot entity trees per
  credential type — rather than threading the type through a single
  shared projection. Also covers the lock-side slot mapping question:
  Z-Wave User Credential CC indexes each (user_id, credential_type,
  credential_slot) independently, so a given LCM "slot N" can hold a
  PIN at one lock-side index and a PASSWORD at another lock-side
  index. Need to decide whether LCM enforces `lock_slot = user_id =
  LCM_slot_N` for every credential type (simplest, may waste lock-side
  slot capacity for sparse type usage), or maintains a richer mapping
  table per type (more flexible, more state to track and recover at
  setup). Re-evaluate when adding the password credential type per the
  expansion entry above — that's the first time the question has a
  concrete answer to validate against.

# TODO

## User-Facing Features

- **Config flow: conflicting integration detection** — Warn if Keymaster or other
  code management integrations are detected at setup.
- **Clear all unmanaged codes** — Button or service to clear unmanaged code slots
  on a lock.
- **Bulk operations** — Enable/disable multiple slots at once.
- **Import/export slot configuration**
- **PIN validation** — Warn on simple PINs ("1234", "0000"), duplicate PINs
  across slots, format violations against lock requirements, and slot conflicts
  across config entries.
- **Service actions:**
  - `generate_pin` — auto-generate secure PIN
- **Custom Jinja templates** — Ship `.jinja` macros for LCM entity resolution
  (e.g. `lcm_slot_entities(config_entry_id, slot_num)`). Auto-install to
  `custom_templates/` during setup.

## Providers

**Future:** Nuki, SimpliSafe (has `async_get_pins()` in simplipy — full
read/write, name-based, max 4 user PINs), SwitchBot, SmartThings

**Cannot support:** esphome (no API), august/yale/yalexs_ble/yale_smart_alarm
(library limitations), ISY994 (pyisy has no code read support)

### Matter provider

- **Known Aqara U300 limitations** (discovered during live testing 2026-04-18):
  - User names with spaces rejected (500 error from `set_lock_user`)
  - Status `unknown(133)` when setting credential on occupied slot without clearing
  - Lock disconnects from Thread network after repeated HA restarts (needs battery
    pull + Matter server restart to recover)

## Architecture Considerations

- **Event-driven vs optimistic push updates** — Matter, Z-Wave JS, and
  Zigbee2MQTT all need optimistic pushes from set/clear methods. Z-Wave JS
  needs them to avoid sync loops with stale cache reads. Matter needs them
  because PINs are write-only. Zigbee2MQTT uses MQTT QoS 0 which has no
  delivery confirmation. Removing optimistic pushes is not viable for any
  push-based provider. Event-only updates would leave a latency window where
  the coordinator has stale data, triggering unnecessary re-sync attempts.

- **Coordinator-owned sync managers** — Move sync manager lifecycle from binary
  sensor entities to coordinator (survives entity recreation during config
  updates).

- **Expand sync "in sync" predicate to include user name + every managed
  credential type** — Today sync compares PIN only. With names (User
  Credential CC) and future credential types (PASSWORD, etc.), a slot
  should be "out of sync" if any field — name, PIN, or any managed
  non-PIN credential — drifts from config. Smart writes only the deltas:
  name-only via `async_set_user`, credential-only via `async_set_credential`
  per mismatching type, both via `_put_credential` for the create path.
  Trivially-equal name comparison on User Code CC locks (both sides
  None) keeps the same code path safe for legacy locks. Requires the
  coordinator/sync state to carry `User` rather than `dict[int,
  SlotCredential]` so name is visible to the sync predicate.

- **Least-common-denominator capability aggregation across locks in a
  config entry** — When a config entry covers multiple locks, aggregate
  per-credential-type limits as the LCD so a single configured value
  works on all of them. Numeric bounds: `min_length = max(...)`,
  `max_length = min(...)` over locks that support the type. Skip locks
  where the capability doesn't apply (e.g. User Code CC locks contribute
  nothing to name-length aggregation because UC has no names). Type
  availability is disjunctive (any lock supports PASSWORD → field
  appears; the sync layer per-lock decides whether to write).
  Failure modes to design: empty aggregate (min > max — fail-loud at
  setup with a config error, suggest splitting locks into separate
  entries); recompute on lock add/remove with re-validation of existing
  slot configs. Enforce at the `text` entity level via `native_min` /
  `native_max` for both PIN and name. Lands after the password
  expansion + name-reconciliation sync work above.

- **Expand beyond PIN: add password credential type** — Z-Wave User
  Credential CC and Matter DoorLock both expose `PASSWORD` natively.
  Slot config schema becomes `{name, pin, password, ...}` — slot per
  user, multiple credential types per slot. Capability-gated per slot
  by `max_user_name_length > 0` (real users) and
  `credential_types[PASSWORD]` advertised by at least one lock in the
  entry. Base orchestration is already type-parametric
  (`_project_users_to_slots(credential_type)`); the work is config
  schema, frontend (separate text entity per type per slot or
  capability-driven dropdown), and sync layer multiplexing per type.
  Sequence: lands after the sync-state expansion above and the LCD
  aggregation, since both are prerequisites for a coherent multi-type
  UX.

## Code Quality

- **Dual storage pattern** — Simplify `data` + `options` config entry pattern.
  Document when to use each.
- **TypedDict for slot config** — Define `class SlotConfig(TypedDict)` with
  `pin: NotRequired[str]`, `enabled: bool`, `name: NotRequired[str]`,
  `entity_id: NotRequired[str]` to replace `dict[str, Any]` for slot inner
  dicts. Gives pyright real signal on slot reads/writes. Would let
  `EntryConfig.slots` be typed `Mapping[int, SlotConfig]`.
- **`coordinator.data` typing** — Currently `dict[int, str | SlotCode]`,
  already int-keyed. `binary_sensor.py:281` and `entity.py:193` cast
  defensively against `self.slot_num`'s type variance — could be cleaned
  up once entities consistently carry int slot_num end-to-end.
- **Other internal dict boundaries** — `websocket.py:401/442/524/529`
  cast into websocket-internal lookup dicts; `__init__.py:561` casts at
  a callback boundary. Same broader "typed slot_num everywhere" theme as
  the EntryConfig migration; resolved by the SlotConfig TypedDict work
  above plus typed callback signatures.
- **Entity registry change detection** — Warn if LCM entity IDs change (reload
  required).

## Testing

- **Live testing** — Remaining scenarios not yet validated on any lock:
  - Multiple config entries sharing the same lock (conflict detection)
  - Hard refresh drift detection (verify poll catches out-of-band changes)
  - Config flow re-add (picks up existing codes on lock)

## Frontend

- Test visual editor for both cards (post-redesign).

## Process

- `CLAUDE.md` points to `AGENTS.md`; update `AGENTS.md` after architecture
  changes.
- Review TODOs after completing current work, when starting new features, during
  refactoring sessions, or on `/todos`.

input.old_string:

input.new_string:
_ZWAVE_TO_DOMAIN_CREDENTIAL_TYPE: dict[UserCredentialType, CredentialType] = {
    UserCredentialType.PIN_CODE: CredentialType.PIN,
    UserCredentialType.PASSWORD: CredentialType.PASSWORD,
    UserCredentialType.RFID_CODE: CredentialType.RFID,
    UserCredentialType.NFC: CredentialType.NFC,
    UserCredentialType.FACE_BIOMETRIC: CredentialType.FACE,
    UserCredentialType.FINGER_BIOMETRIC: CredentialType.FINGERPRINT,
}

# Inverse: lock_helpers reports supported_credential_types keyed by the
# string lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP yields. Map those strings back to
# the domain enum so async_get_capabilities can record the full set the
# lock advertises (used by ``_uses_user_code_cc``).
_DOMAIN_CREDENTIAL_TYPE_BY_STR: dict[str, CredentialType] = {
    lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP[zw_type]: domain_type
    for zw_type, domain_type in _ZWAVE_TO_DOMAIN_CREDENTIAL_TYPE.items()
    if zw_type in lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP
}

content.text: You're right — they overlap. Let me merge them into one entry that covers both the feature and the architectural decision it forces.

input.old_string:
- **Option B: entity-type scoping per credential type ("slot mappings")**
  — Today the base orchestration carries Option A (parametric per-type
  projection via `_project_users_to_slots(credential_type)`). The
  open question is whether the coordinator/entities themselves should
  become type-scoped from the top — separate slot entity trees per
  credential type — rather than threading the type through a single
  shared projection. Also covers the lock-side slot mapping question:
  Z-Wave User Credential CC indexes each (user_id, credential_type,
  credential_slot) independently, so a given LCM "slot N" can hold a
  PIN at one lock-side index and a PASSWORD at another lock-side
  index. Need to decide whether LCM enforces `lock_slot = user_id =
  LCM_slot_N` for every credential type (simplest, may waste lock-side
  slot capacity for sparse type usage), or maintains a richer mapping
  table per type (more flexible, more state to track and recover at
  setup). Re-evaluate when adding the password credential type per the
  expansion entry above — that's the first time the question has a
  concrete answer to validate against.

- **Expand beyond PIN: add password credential type** — Z-Wave User
  Credential CC and Matter DoorLock both expose `PASSWORD` natively.
  Slot config schema becomes `{name, pin, password, ...}` — slot per
  user, multiple credential types per slot. Capability-gated per slot
  by `max_user_name_length > 0` (real users) and
  `credential_types[PASSWORD]` advertised by at least one lock in the
  entry. Base orchestration is already type-parametric
  (`_project_users_to_slots(credential_type)`); the work is config
  schema, frontend (separate text entity per type per slot or
  capability-driven dropdown), and sync layer multiplexing per type.
  Sequence: lands after the sync-state expansion above and the LCD
  aggregation, since both are prerequisites for a coherent multi-type
  UX.
input.new_string:
- **Multi-credential-type support: password (Option B architecture
  decision)** — Z-Wave User Credential CC and Matter DoorLock both
  expose `PASSWORD` natively; this is the first concrete second type
  beyond PIN. Sequence-wise lands after the sync-state expansion above
  and the LCD aggregation, since both are prerequisites for a coherent
  multi-type UX.
  The feature work: slot config schema becomes `{name, pin, password,
  ...}` (slot per user, multiple credential types per slot);
  capability-gated per slot by `max_user_name_length > 0` and at least
  one lock advertising `credential_types[PASSWORD]`; sync layer
  multiplexes the write/clear per type.
  The architecture decision (Option B): the base orchestration already
  carries Option A (parametric per-type projection via
  `_project_users_to_slots(credential_type)`) so the seam supports
  adding types additively. Open question is whether the
  coordinator/entities also become type-scoped from the top — separate
  slot entity trees per credential type — or whether a single slot
  surfaces multiple type fields. Tied up with the lock-side slot
  mapping: Z-Wave User Credential CC indexes (user_id,
  credential_type, credential_slot) independently, so LCM "slot N"
  could hold PIN at one lock-side index and PASSWORD at another.
  Simplest is to enforce `lock_slot = user_id = LCM_slot_N` for every
  credential type (matches the existing 1:1:1 invariant, may waste
  lock-side slot capacity when types are sparsely used); richer is a
  per-type mapping table (more flexibility, more state to track and
  recover at setup). Decide the entity-tree shape and the slot-mapping
  rule together when wiring password — they're one design question
  with two surfaces.

  with two surfaces.
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# TODO

## User-Facing Features

- **Config flow: conflicting integration detection** — Warn if Keymaster or other
  code management integrations are detected at setup.
- **Clear all unmanaged codes** — Button or service to clear unmanaged code slots
  on a lock.
- **Bulk operations** — Enable/disable multiple slots at once.
- **Import/export slot configuration**
- **PIN validation** — Warn on simple PINs ("1234", "0000"), duplicate PINs
  across slots, format violations against lock requirements, and slot conflicts
  across config entries.
- **Service actions:**
  - `generate_pin` — auto-generate secure PIN
- **Custom Jinja templates** — Ship `.jinja` macros for LCM entity resolution
  (e.g. `lcm_slot_entities(config_entry_id, slot_num)`). Auto-install to
  `custom_templates/` during setup.

## Providers

**Future:** Nuki, SimpliSafe (has `async_get_pins()` in simplipy — full
read/write, name-based, max 4 user PINs), SwitchBot, SmartThings

**Cannot support:** esphome (no API), august/yale/yalexs_ble/yale_smart_alarm
(library limitations), ISY994 (pyisy has no code read support)

### Matter provider

- **Known Aqara U300 limitations** (discovered during live testing 2026-04-18):
  - User names with spaces rejected (500 error from `set_lock_user`)
  - Status `unknown(133)` when setting credential on occupied slot without clearing
  - Lock disconnects from Thread network after repeated HA restarts (needs battery
    pull + Matter server restart to recover)

## Architecture Considerations

- **Event-driven vs optimistic push updates** — Matter, Z-Wave JS, and
  Zigbee2MQTT all need optimistic pushes from set/clear methods. Z-Wave JS
  needs them to avoid sync loops with stale cache reads. Matter needs them
  because PINs are write-only. Zigbee2MQTT uses MQTT QoS 0 which has no
  delivery confirmation. Removing optimistic pushes is not viable for any
  push-based provider. Event-only updates would leave a latency window where
  the coordinator has stale data, triggering unnecessary re-sync attempts.

- **Coordinator-owned sync managers** — Move sync manager lifecycle from binary
  sensor entities to coordinator (survives entity recreation during config
  updates).

- **Expand sync "in sync" predicate to include user name + every managed
  credential type** — Today sync compares PIN only. With names (User
  Credential CC) and future credential types (PASSWORD, etc.), a slot
  should be "out of sync" if any field — name, PIN, or any managed
  non-PIN credential — drifts from config. Smart writes only the deltas:
  name-only via `async_set_user`, credential-only via `async_set_credential`
  per mismatching type, both via `_put_credential` for the create path.
  Trivially-equal name comparison on User Code CC locks (both sides
  None) keeps the same code path safe for legacy locks. Requires the
  coordinator/sync state to carry `User` rather than `dict[int,
  SlotCredential]` so name is visible to the sync predicate.

- **Least-common-denominator capability aggregation across locks in a
  config entry** — When a config entry covers multiple locks, aggregate
  per-credential-type limits as the LCD so a single configured value
  works on all of them. Rules per capability kind:
  - Numeric bounds: `min_length = max(...)`, `max_length = min(...)`
    across the locks that support the type. Skip locks where the
    capability doesn't apply (e.g. User Code CC locks contribute
    nothing to name-length aggregation because UC has no names).
  - Type availability: any lock supports the type → the field appears
    on the slot config; the sync layer per-lock decides whether to
    write. When no lock supports the type, hide the field entirely.
  - Boolean feature flags (e.g. `supports_learn` for RFID): AND across
    the supporting locks.
  Empty aggregate (e.g. PIN min > max because one lock requires <=6 and
  another requires >=8) must be a hard config flow failure surfaced in
  both the initial config flow and the option flow ("these locks have
  incompatible PIN length requirements; split them into separate config
  entries"). On lock add/remove, re-validate existing slot configs
  against the recomputed aggregate and disable any slots whose stored
  values fall outside the new bounds, with a clear repair-suggestion
  notification. Enforce at the `text` entity level via `native_min` /
  `native_max` for both PIN and name. Lands after the password
  expansion + name-reconciliation sync work above.

- **Option B: entity-type scoping per credential type ("slot mappings")**
  — Today the base orchestration carries Option A (parametric per-type
  projection via `_project_users_to_slots(credential_type)`). The
  open question is whether the coordinator/entities themselves should
  become type-scoped from the top — separate slot entity trees per
  credential type — rather than threading the type through a single
  shared projection. Also covers the lock-side slot mapping question:
  Z-Wave User Credential CC indexes each (user_id, credential_type,
  credential_slot) independently, so a given LCM "slot N" can hold a
  PIN at one lock-side index and a PASSWORD at another lock-side
  index. Need to decide whether LCM enforces `lock_slot = user_id =
  LCM_slot_N` for every credential type (simplest, may waste lock-side
  slot capacity for sparse type usage), or maintains a richer mapping
  table per type (more flexible, more state to track and recover at
  setup). Re-evaluate when adding the password credential type per the
  expansion entry above — that's the first time the question has a
  concrete answer to validate against.

- **Expand beyond PIN: add password credential type** — Z-Wave User
  Credential CC and Matter DoorLock both expose `PASSWORD` natively.
  Slot config schema becomes `{name, pin, password, ...}` — slot per
  user, multiple credential types per slot. Capability-gated per slot
  by `max_user_name_length > 0` (real users) and
  `credential_types[PASSWORD]` advertised by at least one lock in the
  entry. Base orchestration is already type-parametric
  (`_project_users_to_slots(credential_type)`); the work is config
  schema, frontend (separate text entity per type per slot or
  capability-driven dropdown), and sync layer multiplexing per type.
  Sequence: lands after the sync-state expansion above and the LCD
  aggregation, since both are prerequisites for a coherent multi-type
  UX.

## Code Quality

- **Dual storage pattern** — Simplify `data` + `options` config entry pattern.
  Document when to use each.
- **TypedDict for slot config** — Define `class SlotConfig(TypedDict)` with
  `pin: NotRequired[str]`, `enabled: bool`, `name: NotRequired[str]`,
  `entity_id: NotRequired[str]` to replace `dict[str, Any]` for slot inner
  dicts. Gives pyright real signal on slot reads/writes. Would let
  `EntryConfig.slots` be typed `Mapping[int, SlotConfig]`.
- **`coordinator.data` typing** — Currently `dict[int, str | SlotCode]`,
  already int-keyed. `binary_sensor.py:281` and `entity.py:193` cast
  defensively against `self.slot_num`'s type variance — could be cleaned
  up once entities consistently carry int slot_num end-to-end.
- **Other internal dict boundaries** — `websocket.py:401/442/524/529`
  cast into websocket-internal lookup dicts; `__init__.py:561` casts at
  a callback boundary. Same broader "typed slot_num everywhere" theme as
  the EntryConfig migration; resolved by the SlotConfig TypedDict work
  above plus typed callback signatures.
- **Entity registry change detection** — Warn if LCM entity IDs change (reload
  required).

## Testing

- **Live testing** — Remaining scenarios not yet validated on any lock:
  - Multiple config entries sharing the same lock (conflict detection)
  - Hard refresh drift detection (verify poll catches out-of-band changes)
  - Config flow re-add (picks up existing codes on lock)

## Frontend

- Test visual editor for both cards (post-redesign).

## Process

- `CLAUDE.md` points to `AGENTS.md`; update `AGENTS.md` after architecture
  changes.
- Review TODOs after completing current work, when starting new features, during
  refactoring sessions, or on `/todos`.

async def test_setup_is_idempotent(
    hass: HomeAssistant,
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    lock_code_manager_config_entry: MockConfigEntry,
) -> None:
    """Test that async_setup clears old listeners before re-registering."""
    await zwave_js_lock.async_setup(lock_code_manager_config_entry)
    assert len(zwave_js_lock._listeners) >= 1
    count_after_first = len(zwave_js_lock._listeners)

    # Call again -- should not accumulate listeners.
    await zwave_js_lock.async_setup(lock_code_manager_config_entry)
    assert len(zwave_js_lock._listeners) == count_after_first

When the lazy max-name-length lookup raises, async_set_user proceeds with a
    max length of 0 (names omitted) for this call but leaves the cache unset so
    the next call retries the lookup. Caching a 0 sentinel on failure would
    silently strip all user names for the provider instance after a single
    transient disconnect.
    """
    mock_lock_helpers[
        "async_get_credential_capabilities"
    ].side_effect = FailedZWaveCommand("cmd", 1, "caps unavailable")
    mock_access_control.get_user_cached.return_value = None

    user = User(user_id=1, name="alice", active=True)
    result = await zwave_js_lock.async_set_user(user)

    assert result == SetUserResult(user_id=1, created=True)
    # Cache stays None on failure so the next call retries; the failed read
    # only suppresses the name guard for THIS call.
    assert zwave_js_lock._max_user_name_length is None
    # max length 0 → the name is omitted on this call
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].assert_called_once_with(
        zwave_js_lock.node,
        user_id=1,
        user_name=None,
        active=True,
    )


async def test_async_set_user_retries_capabilities_after_failure(
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    mock_access_control: MagicMock,
    mock_lock_helpers: dict,
) -> None:
    """
    After a transient capabilities-fetch failure, the next call retries.

    Regression guard for the bug where caching a 0 sentinel on failure would
    permanently disable user-name validation until the integration reloaded.
    """
    mock_lock_helpers["async_get_credential_capabilities"].side_effect = [
        FailedZWaveCommand("cmd", 1, "caps unavailable"),
        {
            "max_user_name_length": 10,
            "supports_user_management": True,
            "max_users": 5,
            "supported_credential_types": {},

async def test_setup_is_idempotent(
    hass: HomeAssistant,
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    lock_code_manager_config_entry: MockConfigEntry,
) -> None:
    """Test that async_setup clears old listeners before re-registering."""
    await zwave_js_lock.async_setup(lock_code_manager_config_entry)
    assert len(zwave_js_lock._listeners) >= 1
    count_after_first = len(zwave_js_lock._listeners)

    # Call again -- should not accumulate listeners.
    await zwave_js_lock.async_setup(lock_code_manager_config_entry)
    assert len(zwave_js_lock._listeners) == count_after_first


# Connection tests


async def test_is_integration_connected_when_loaded(
    hass: HomeAssistant,
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    zwave_integration: MockConfigEntry,
) -> None:
    """Test connection is up when config entry is loaded and client connected."""
    assert zwave_integration.state == ConfigEntryState.LOADED
    assert await zwave_js_lock.async_is_integration_connected() is True


async def test_is_integration_not_connected_when_not_loaded(
    hass: HomeAssistant,
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    zwave_integration: MockConfigEntry,
) -> None:
    """Test connection is down when config entry not loaded."""
    await hass.config_entries.async_unload(zwave_integration.entry_id)
    await hass.async_block_till_done()

    assert zwave_integration.state != ConfigEntryState.LOADED
    assert await zwave_js_lock.async_is_integration_connected() is False

async def test_async_set_user_omits_name_when_max_name_length_is_zero(
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    mock_access_control: MagicMock,
    mock_lock_helpers: dict,
) -> None:
    """When max_user_name_length is 0, user_name is passed as None."""
    pin_type_str = lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP[UserCredentialType.PIN_CODE]
    mock_lock_helpers["async_get_credential_capabilities"].return_value = {
        "supports_user_management": True,
        "max_users": 30,
        "supported_user_types": [],
        "max_user_name_length": 0,
        "supported_credential_rules": [],
        "supported_credential_types": {
            pin_type_str: {
                "num_slots": 30,
                "min_length": 4,
                "max_length": 8,
                "supports_learn": False,
            }
        },
    }
    mock_access_control.get_user_cached.return_value = None
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].return_value = {"user_id": 5}

    # Name is provided, but the lock doesn't support names → pass None
    user = User(user_id=5, name="alice", active=True)
    result = await zwave_js_lock.async_set_user(user)

    assert result == SetUserResult(user_id=5, created=True)
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].assert_called_once_with(
        zwave_js_lock.node,
        user_id=5,
        user_name=None,
        active=True,
    )


async def test_async_set_user_truncates_name_when_too_long(
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    mock_access_control: MagicMock,
    mock_lock_helpers: dict,
) -> None:
    """When user name exceeds max_user_name_length, it is truncated."""
    pin_type_str = lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP[UserCredentialType.PIN_CODE]
    mock_lock_helpers["async_get_credential_capabilities"].return_value = {
        "supports_user_management": True,
        "max_users": 30,
        "supported_user_types": [],
        "max_user_name_length": 3,
        "supported_credential_rules": [],
        "supported_credential_types": {
            pin_type_str: {
                "num_slots": 30,
                "min_length": 4,
                "max_length": 8,
                "supports_learn": False,
            }
        },
    }
    mock_access_control.get_user_cached.return_value = None
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].return_value = {"user_id": 5}

    # Name "alice" (5 chars) truncated to "ali" (3 chars)
    user = User(user_id=5, name="alice", active=True)
    result = await zwave_js_lock.async_set_user(user)

    assert result == SetUserResult(user_id=5, created=True)
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].assert_called_once_with(
        zwave_js_lock.node,
        user_id=5,
        user_name="ali",
        active=True,
    )

async def test_async_set_user_skips_driver_call_for_user_code_cc_lock(
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    mock_access_control: MagicMock,
    mock_lock_helpers: dict,
) -> None:
    """
    On a User Code CC lock, async_set_user is a no-op.

    The unified accessControl API's setUser cannot run before a credential
    exists on a UC lock (the user IS the code), so the seam's "set user
    first, then credential" sequence has to skip the set-user write. The
    subsequent async_set_credential creates the user implicitly. We
    return ``created=False`` because there is no separate user record
    for _put_credential to roll back if the credential write fails.

    Detection signal: ``max_user_name_length == 0`` AND only PIN in
    ``supported_credential_types`` — the conjunction is the practical
    User Code CC indicator.
    """
    pin_type_str = lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP[UserCredentialType.PIN_CODE]
    mock_lock_helpers["async_get_credential_capabilities"].return_value = {
        "supports_user_management": True,
        "max_users": 30,
        "supported_user_types": [],
        "max_user_name_length": 0,
        "supported_credential_rules": [],
        "supported_credential_types": {
            pin_type_str: {
                "num_slots": 30,
                "min_length": 4,
                "max_length": 8,
                "supports_learn": False,
            }
        },
    }

    user = User(user_id=5, name="alice", active=True)
    result = await zwave_js_lock.async_set_user(user)

    assert result == SetUserResult(user_id=5, created=False)
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].assert_not_called()


async def test_async_set_user_calls_driver_for_user_credential_cc_with_no_name_support(
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    mock_access_control: MagicMock,
    mock_lock_helpers: dict,
) -> None:
    """
    A degenerate U3C lock with ``max_user_name_length == 0`` but multi-type
    support is NOT treated as User Code CC -- the driver call still goes
    through with ``user_name=None``.

    Guards against an over-broad UC heuristic: max_user_name_length alone
    is not enough; the credential-type set must also be PIN-only.
    """
    pin_type_str = lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP[UserCredentialType.PIN_CODE]
    rfid_type_str = lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP[UserCredentialType.RFID_CODE]
    mock_lock_helpers["async_get_credential_capabilities"].return_value = {
        "supports_user_management": True,
        "max_users": 30,
        "supported_user_types": [],
        "max_user_name_length": 0,
        "supported_credential_rules": [],
        "supported_credential_types": {
            pin_type_str: {
                "num_slots": 30,
                "min_length": 4,
                "max_length": 8,
                "supports_learn": False,
            },
            rfid_type_str: {
                "num_slots": 30,
                "min_length": 4,
                "max_length": 16,
                "supports_learn": False,
            },
        },
    }
    mock_access_control.get_user_cached.return_value = None
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].return_value = {"user_id": 5}

    user = User(user_id=5, name="alice", active=True)
    result = await zwave_js_lock.async_set_user(user)

    assert result == SetUserResult(user_id=5, created=True)
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].assert_called_once_with(
        zwave_js_lock.node,
        user_id=5,
        user_name=None,
        active=True,
    )

    return ``created=False`` because there is no separate user record
    for _put_credential to roll back if the credential write fails.

    Detection signal: ``max_user_name_length == 0`` AND only PIN in
    ``supported_credential_types`` — the conjunction is the practical
    User Code CC indicator.
    """
    pin_type_str = lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP[UserCredentialType.PIN_CODE]
    mock_lock_helpers["async_get_credential_capabilities"].return_value = {
        "supports_user_management": True,
        "max_users": 30,
        "supported_user_types": [],
        "max_user_name_length": 0,
        "supported_credential_rules": [],
        "supported_credential_types": {
            pin_type_str: {
                "num_slots": 30,
                "min_length": 4,
                "max_length": 8,
                "supports_learn": False,
            }
        },
    }

    user = User(user_id=5, name="alice", active=True)
    result = await zwave_js_lock.async_set_user(user)

    assert result == SetUserResult(user_id=5, created=False)
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].assert_not_called()


async def test_async_set_user_calls_driver_for_user_credential_cc_with_no_name_support(
    zwave_js_lock: ZWaveJSLock,
    mock_access_control: MagicMock,
    mock_lock_helpers: dict,
) -> None:
    """
    A degenerate U3C lock with ``max_user_name_length == 0`` but multi-type
    support is NOT treated as User Code CC -- the driver call still goes
    through with ``user_name=None``.

    Guards against an over-broad UC heuristic: max_user_name_length alone
    is not enough; the credential-type set must also be PIN-only.
    """
    pin_type_str = lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP[UserCredentialType.PIN_CODE]
    rfid_type_str = lock_helpers.CREDENTIAL_TYPE_MAP[UserCredentialType.RFID_CODE]
    mock_lock_helpers["async_get_credential_capabilities"].return_value = {
        "supports_user_management": True,
        "max_users": 30,
        "supported_user_types": [],
        "max_user_name_length": 0,
        "supported_credential_rules": [],
        "supported_credential_types": {
            pin_type_str: {
                "num_slots": 30,
                "min_length": 4,
                "max_length": 8,
                "supports_learn": False,
            },
            rfid_type_str: {
                "num_slots": 30,
                "min_length": 4,
                "max_length": 16,
                "supports_learn": False,
            },
        },
    }
    mock_access_control.get_user_cached.return_value = None
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].return_value = {"user_id": 5}

    user = User(user_id=5, name="alice", active=True)
    result = await zwave_js_lock.async_set_user(user)

    assert result == SetUserResult(user_id=5, created=True)
    mock_lock_helpers["async_set_user"].assert_called_once_with(
        zwave_js_lock.node,
        user_id=5,
        user_name=None,
        active=True,
    )

225	
226	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
227	class LockCapabilities:
228	    """
229	    What a lock can do, as a platform-neutral snapshot.
230	
231	    ``supports_user_management`` mirrors the providers' existing gate;
232	    ``max_users`` is the total number of users the lock can hold; and
233	    ``credential_types`` maps each supported ``CredentialType`` to its
234	    per-type limits. A type absent from the mapping is unsupported.
235	    """
236	
237	    supports_user_management: bool
238	    max_users: int
239	    credential_types: Mapping[CredentialType, CredentialTypeCapability]
240	
241	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
242	        """Snapshot credential_types so the value object cannot be mutated later."""
243	        object.__setattr__(
244	            self,
245	            "credential_types",
246	            MappingProxyType(dict(self.credential_types)),
247	        )
248	
249	    def capability_for(
250	        self, credential_type: CredentialType
251	    ) -> CredentialTypeCapability | None:
252	        """Return the per-type limits for ``credential_type``, else ``None``."""
253	        return self.credential_types.get(credential_type)
254	
255	    def supports(self, credential_type: CredentialType) -> bool:
256	        """Return True when the lock advertises ``credential_type``."""
257	        return credential_type in self.credential_types

class GetLockInfoResult(TypedDict):
    """Result of get_lock_info service action."""

    supports_user_management: bool
    supported_credential_types: list[str]
    max_users: int | None
    max_pin_users: int | None
